item.title: Vibe Collab: a way to collaborate while wielding AI agents
item.author: renich
item.content:encoded: I am opening this topic so that we can discuss this.We should create a protocol to enable sane collaboration while using AI agents.What that protocol should be.How does practical collaboration among developers, AI agents and core developers look like?What is practical/impractical?What speeds up development, review and integration?What are the compromises?

item.title: Why Isn't Every Sane Developer Obsessed with Crystal?
item.author: omarluq
item.content:encoded: Seriously. This keeps me up at night. The language is right there. It’s fast, it’s beautiful, it’s expressive, the type system is incredible, and it compiles to native binaries. What more do people want?I watch developers mass migrate to languages that make them fight the compiler, write verbose boilerplate, or handle errors like it’s 1995, and I just don’t get it. Crystal is something genuinely special and the rest of the world just walks right past it.What is it going to take?10 posts - 5 participants

item.title: The mighty Tsoding made a crystal video
item.author: renich
item.content:encoded: I dunno if you follow this guy. I do. He’s a master programmer.Well, he made a YouTube video 5 years ago to try out crystal.Haven’t seen it yet but I was gratiously amazed.

item.title: Need to crazy-optimize some GMP stuff?
item.author: ralsina
item.content:encoded:
While crystal stdlib has a GMP binding (The Big Module) which is super convenient to use, what happens when you need something NOT convenient to use but faster?The main bottleneck in Big’s performance is that they are immutable, so super tight loops with operations on Bigs will pay the object allocation tax.require "mut_gmp"
a = MutGMP::MpZ.new(42) b = MutGMP::MpZ.new(10)
a.add!(b) # a is now 52, mutated in-place a.mul!(2) # a is now 104
# Operations can be chained c = MutGMP::MpZ.new(5) c.add!(10).mul!(2).sub!(5) # c is now 25

item.title: ANSI sequences modified unexpectedly
item.author: hutou
item.content:encoded: In a specification source code, I use ANSI sequences to surround certain values in order to check if they are rendered correctly. Several months ago (March 2025), when I wrote this code, the specifications worked correctly. Today, they fail (Crystal 1.19.1) and I noticed something strange: in the displayed failures, the sequence in my source code (the “expected” value) is replaced by the sequence (the “obtained” value), which of course produces an error. I don’t understand why!

item.title: Di; new dependency injection shard
item.author: omarluq
item.content:encoded:
I just released Di v0.1.0, a new dependency injection shard.Di is macro first and type safe. It supports auto wire from constructor types, named services, transient services, fiber local scopes, health checks, and graceful shutdown.require "di"
Di.provide { Database.new(ENV["DATABASE_URL"]) } Di.provide UserRepo Di.provide UserService
svc = Di.invoke(UserService) # or svc = Di[UserService]
Di.scope(:request) do Di.provide { CurrentUser.from_token(token) } current = Di.invoke(CurrentUser) # or current = Di[CurrentUser] end Feedback are always welcome.18 posts - 5 participants
